tags: 強い vs 弱い pair
lesson:
酒さけに弱よわい — weak against alcohol, a lightweight. This pattern — Xに弱い — is one of the most useful things about this word. It means you're susceptible to X, you can't handle it. 暑あつさに弱よわい (bad with heat). プレッシャーに弱よわい (cracks under pressure). The に marks what defeats you.

After a number, 弱 means "slightly less than": 三十分弱さんじゅっぷんじゃく (a little under thirty minutes). Its opposite 強きょう means "slightly more than": 百人強ひゃくにんきょう (just over 100 people). This is a genuinely useful trick that no dictionary definition of "weak" would prepare you for.

The kanji 弱 shows two bows (弓ゆみ) with decorative marks — a bow that's ornamental rather than functional. It looks like a weapon but can't shoot. The appearance of strength without the substance.

Dictionaryよわい
Negativeよわくない
Pastよわかった
Past negよわくなかった
Adverbよわく
て-formよわくて

耳みみが痛いたい doesn't always mean your ear hurts. It can mean something you heard was painfully true — criticism that hit home. 耳みみにする (to happen to hear something). 耳みみが早はやい (to have fast ears — to hear news quickly, to be well-informed). The ear in Japanese is the organ of being alert, being connected to the world.

The kanji 耳 is one of the most faithful pictographs surviving into modern writing. The oracle bone version was a drawing of an ear, and the modern form still looks like one — the outer rectangle is the ear shape, the inner horizontal lines are the curves of the canal. It's also a radical in other kanji: 聞ぶん (to hear/ask — an ear at a gate), 聴ちょう (to listen carefully).

Sound anchor: mi-mi — the doubled sound is itself worth noting. Japanese has several body-part words with repeated syllables: 耳 (みみ, ear), 目め (eye — not doubled, but paired with 耳 constantly). The two senses appear together in phrases like 目めと耳みみを使つかう (use your eyes and ears — pay close attention).

Before chairs arrived from the West, Japan sat on the floor. 座る originally meant exactly that — settling onto the ground or a cushion, folding your legs underneath you. The kanji 座 shows people (人ひと人ひと) on earth (土つち) under a roof (广). People sitting on the ground inside a building. That's what sitting looked like for most of Japanese history.

Modern 座る works for chairs too: 椅子いすに座すわる (sit in a chair). ここに座すわってください (please sit here). But the floor-sitting origin persists in vocabulary: 正座せいざ (formal kneeling — "correct sitting"), the legs-folded-underneath position that numbs your feet after ten minutes. 座布団ざぶとん (floor cushion) — the cushion that makes floor-sitting bearable.

Particles: 座る takes に — the location where you sit. 椅子いすに座すわる. 床ゆかに座すわる (sit on the floor).

Passive に座られる: に marks the sitter. 隣となりに座すわられた (someone sat next to me — and I didn't want them to). Adversative passive — their sitting affected you.

Causative を／に座らせる: を leans coercive. 子供こどもを椅子いすに座すわらせる (make the kid sit in the chair).

The kanji 右 is a hand (ナ) plus a mouth (口くち). A hand reaching toward the mouth — and since most people eat with their right hand, that hand became "right." Its twin 左ひだり (left) has a hand plus a tool-like shape — the hand that holds instruments. Right eats, left works. The ancient division of labor is frozen in the characters.

右みぎに曲まがる (turn right). 右手みぎて (right hand). 右側みぎがわ (right side). In Japan, traffic drives on the left, pedestrians keep left on escalators in Osaka (but right in Tokyo — a regional quirk that confuses even Japanese people). Direction words get daily use.

右 in compounds read as う carries political or metaphorical weight: 右翼うよく (right wing — political). 左右さゆう (left and right — and also "influence," as in 結果けっかを左右さゆうする, to influence an outcome, to sway it left and right).

浴びる doesn't mean "to take a bath" — that's おふろに入る. 浴びる means to have something pour over you, to be drenched in it. The most common use: シャワーを浴あびる (take a shower). But it extends beyond water: 日光にっこうを浴あびる (bathe in sunlight). 注目ちゅうもくを浴あびる (bathe in attention — be in the spotlight). 批判ひはんを浴あびる (be showered with criticism).

The pattern is always the same: something pours down on you and you receive it with your whole body. Water, light, praise, blame — the image is of standing under a cascade. The kanji 浴よく has 氵 (water) and 谷たに (valley) — water flowing down from a valley onto you.

Particles: 浴びる takes を — the thing pouring over you is the direct object. シャワーを浴あびる. 批判ひはんを浴あびる.

Potential が浴びられる: が. ここで朝日あさひが浴あびられる (you can catch the morning sun here).

Passive に浴びせられる: more commonly, the transitive 浴あびせる (to pour onto someone) is used in passive. 水みずを浴あびせられた (water was poured on me).

Causative に…を浴びさせる: に marks who you drench, を marks what. 子供こどもにシャワーを浴あびさせる (have the kid take a shower).

肩かたがこる — stiff shoulders. This might be the most culturally loaded body complaint in Japan. Where Americans get back pain and Germans get circulation problems, Japanese people get 肩かたこり (shoulder stiffness). It's discussed so universally that some linguists have argued the Japanese experience shoulder tension more because they have a word for it — the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis in action, or just a nation that spends a lot of time hunched over desks.

肩かたの荷にが下おりる (the load on my shoulders came down) — a burden was lifted. 肩かたを持もつ (hold someone's shoulder) — to take someone's side. 肩身かたみが狭せまい (narrow-shouldered feeling) — to feel ashamed or out of place. Shoulders in Japanese carry responsibility, allegiance, and social comfort.

The kanji 肩 has 戸と (door) on top and the flesh radical 月 below. The shoulder as the body's door-frame — the flat platform where arms hinge. Architecturally apt.

もう寝ねる — I'm going to bed. Three syllables, conversation over, goodnight. 寝る covers both going to sleep and lying down. 昨日きのうは早はやく寝ねた (went to bed early). ソファで寝ねていた (was lying on the sofa — maybe asleep, maybe just horizontal).

寝る vs 眠る: 寝る is the physical act — getting into bed, lying down. 眠ねむる is the state of being asleep, the unconsciousness itself. You can 寝る without 眠る (lie in bed unable to sleep). You can 眠る while sitting up on a train. 寝ねても眠ねむれない (I'm in bed but I can't sleep) uses both to make the distinction explicit.

One more: 寝坊ねぼうする (to oversleep) — a compound you'll need. 寝ね (sleep) + 坊ぼう (a suffix that implies someone behaving like a child). Oversleeping as childish indulgence.

Particles: 寝る is intransitive. Use で for where: ベッドで寝ねる. No direct object.

Passive に寝られる: adversative — someone's sleeping affects you. 赤あかちゃんに横よこで寝ねられて動うごけない (the baby fell asleep next to me and I can't move).

Causative を／に寝させる: 子供こどもを早はやく寝ねさせる (put the kid to bed early).

消す makes things disappear. 電気でんきを消けす (turn off the light). テレビを消けす (turn off the TV). 火ひを消けす (put out a fire). 文字もじを消けす (erase text). データを消けす (delete data). The common thread: something that was present — light, sound, flame, information — stops existing. You remove it from the world.

Its intransitive partner 消きえる is when things vanish on their own: 電気でんきが消きえた (the light went out). 雪ゆきが消きえた (the snow melted away). You 消す — you cause the disappearance. Something 消えるs — it fades by itself.

Particles: 消す takes を — the thing you extinguish is the direct object. 電気でんきを消けす.

Potential が消せる: が. このデータが消けせない (I can't delete this data).

Passive に消される: に. 大事だいじなファイルを消けされた (my important file was deleted).

Causative に…を消させる: に marks who you make erase, を marks what. 生徒せいとに黒板こくばんを消けさせる (have the student erase the blackboard).

元気げんきでね — take care. Said when someone is leaving for a long time, carrying a warmth that "goodbye" can't match. It literally means "be well" — stay in your original energy, keep being alive in that buzzing way. This single phrase captures what 元気 really means: not just "fine" but vitally, actively well.

元げん (origin) + 気き (energy/spirit) = original energy. The baseline life force. 元気げんきな子供こども is a kid who won't sit still. 元気げんきがない is the opposite — something's wrong, the spark is out. 元気げんきを出だす (muster energy — pull yourself together, cheer up).

The 気 half of this word is one of the most connected kanji in Japanese. 天気てんき (weather — the sky's energy). 気持きもち (feelings — where energy is held). 人気にんき (popularity — people's energy gathered around something). 気分きぶん (mood — the portion of energy you're feeling right now). These aren't random compounds. They're all variations on a single idea: qi, the invisible force that moves through everything. 元気 is that force at its source.

Dictionaryげんき
Negativeげんきじゃない
Pastげんきだった
Past negげんきじゃなかった
Adverbげんきに
て-formげんきで

全部ぜんぶ食たべた (ate everything). 全部ぜんぶでいくら？ (how much for everything?). 全部 is the blunt, no-remainder "all" — every single part, nothing left out. It works both as a noun and as an adverb, and it's the word you reach for when you want to emphasize totality.

The kanji: 全ぜん (all, complete) + 部ぶ (part, section). All parts. The word is reassuringly literal. 全 shows up in other totality words: 全然ぜんぜん (not at all / completely), 全員ぜんいん (everyone — all members), 全国ぜんこく (nationwide).

全部 vs 全て vs みんな: All mean "everything/all," different flavors. 全部 is the most concrete — every piece, every item. 全すべて is more literary and abstract — "all things." みんな usually means "everyone" (people) but can mean "everything" casually. For physical objects and countable things, 全部 is the default.

去きょ means "to leave, to depart." 年ねん means "year." The year that left — last year. Clean, visual, immediate. 去年きょねんの夏なつ (last summer). 去年きょねんはどうでしたか (how was last year?).

Note the prefix pattern breaks here. For weeks and months, you use 先せん for "last" (先週せんしゅう, 先月せんげつ). For years, it's 去 instead. There's no 先年 in common use. This is one of those places where the system isn't perfectly regular — you just have to know that years are different.

The 去 kanji appears in 過去かこ (the past) — literally "passed and departed." Once something is 去, it's gone. Not coming back.

The kanji 引ひき is a bow (弓ゆみ) with a vertical line — the string pulled taut. Drawing a bowstring is the original act of pulling, and the kanji preserves it perfectly.

引く does more work than English "pull." ドアを引ひく (pull a door). 辞書じしょを引ひく (look up in a dictionary — "pull out" information). 風邪かぜを引ひく (catch a cold — you "draw" the illness to yourself). 線せんを引ひく (draw a line). 引ひく手てあまた (more offers than you can handle — everyone pulling at you). The thread across all uses: bringing something toward you, or drawing something out.

The opposite is 押おす (to push). You'll see 引ひ and 押お on doors everywhere — 引く to pull open, 押す to push open. And 引ひっ越こす (to move house) literally means to pull up and cross over — uprooting yourself.

Particles: 引く takes を — the thing you pull is the direct object. ドアを引ひく. 辞書じしょを引ひく.

Potential が引ける: が. この戸とが引ひけない (I can't pull this door open).

Passive に引かれる: に marks who pulls. 彼かれの才能さいのうに引ひかれた (I was drawn to his talent).

Causative に…を引かせる: に marks who pulls, を marks what. 馬うまに荷車にぐるまを引ひかせる (have the horse pull the cart).

The 館かん suffix is your "building" marker: 映画館えいがかん (movie theater), 美術館びじゅつかん (art museum), 旅館りょかん (Japanese inn). Once you spot 館, you know you're talking about a building with a purpose. 図書館 is 図書としょ (books as a collection) + 館かん (hall). A hall of books.

図書 isn't the word for a single book (that's 本ほん). It implies a collection, an archive — institutional books. 図書としょカード (library card). 図書室としょしつ is a smaller version — a library room in a school, using 室しつ (room) instead of 館 (hall).

図書館としょかんで勉強べんきょうする (study at the library) is a staple of student life. 図書館としょかんの本ほんを借かりる (borrow a library book) — note 借かりる (to borrow), not 貸かす (to lend). You borrow; the library lends.

上げる has two major lives. First: to physically raise something. 手てを上あげる (raise your hand). Its intransitive partner 上あがる is when something rises on its own: 温度おんどが上あがる (the temperature rises). You 上げる things. Things 上がる by themselves.

Second life: to give (to someone of equal or lower status). プレゼントをあげる (give a present). And in the 〜てあげる pattern, it means doing something as a favor: 教おしえてあげる (I'll teach you — as a kindness). This giving sense is so common many learners meet it before the "raise" meaning.

The two aren't disconnected. Giving is an upward motion — you lift something from your side and hand it over. In classical Japanese, offering to a superior meant literally raising it up to them. The physical gesture and the social act share a direction.

Particles: For raising: を. 手てを上あげる. For giving: に marks the recipient, を marks the gift. 友達ともだちにお菓子かしをあげる.

Potential が上げられる: が. 給料きゅうりょうが上あげられない (can't raise the salary).

Passive に上げられる: に. 税金ぜいきんを上あげられた (taxes were raised on us).

Causative に…を上げさせる: に marks who raises, を marks what. 生徒せいとに手てを上あげさせる (have students raise their hands).

Traffic lights in Japan are called 青信号あおしんごう — "blue" signal. They're green. 青野菜あおやさい — "blue" vegetables. They're green. 青々あおあおとした草くさ — lush "blue" grass. Definitely green. What's happening: historically, Japanese 青 covered the entire blue-to-green spectrum. 緑 existed as a separate word, but green was considered a shade of blue, not its own category.

The boundary has been shifting toward the English distinction over the past century, but the old system lingers everywhere. And 緑 is a noun, not an adjective — you can't say 緑い. To modify a noun, use 緑の: 緑みどりの葉は (green leaves). This puts it in a different grammatical class from 赤あかい, 青あおい, 白しろい, 黒くろい — all い-adjectives. Green didn't earn its own adjective form because it wasn't considered a basic color.

The kanji has the silk radical 糸いと — green was a key dye color for silk and fabric. 緑茶りょくちゃ (green tea) uses the on'yomi りょく. 新緑しんりょく (the fresh green of new spring leaves) is a seasonal word that evokes a specific time of year.

腕うでがいい doesn't mean "nice arms." It means "skilled." 腕うでを磨みがく (polish your arms) means to hone your skills. 腕うでの見みせどころ (a place to show your arms) means a chance to show what you can do. 腕前うでまえ means ability level. Your arms are your tools, and what you do with them defines your capability. The figurative meaning is at least as common as the physical one.

For the literal body part: うで means the arm from shoulder to wrist. 腕時計うでどけい (wristwatch — arm-clock). 腕組うでぐみ (folding your arms) — a gesture of thinking, skepticism, or defiance, much as in Western body language.

Three words for "door," each for a different kind. ドア: Western-style, hinged, swings open — houses, cars, building entrances. 戸と: traditional Japanese, slides — the default in older-style rooms. 扉とびら: large, formal — double doors, grand entrances, or metaphorical doors ("the door to a new era"). Your bedroom probably has a ドア. The tatami room has a 戸 or ふすま.

ドアを開あける (open the door). ドアを閉しめる (close the door). ドアをノックする (knock on the door) — ノック is another English loan. Western doors came with Western verbs for using them.

女おんな (woman) + の + 子こ (child) = a female child. Transparent construction. But the age range stretches: a group of women in their twenties might casually call themselves 女の子たち. It carries warmth and youthfulness that 女性じょせい (woman — formal) and 女おんな (woman — blunt) don't.

Choosing wrong has consequences: 女の子 (warm, youthful) → 女おんなの人ひと (neutral polite) → 女性じょせい (formal, respectful) → 女おんな (direct, can sound rude). When in doubt, 女性 is safe. Calling a grown woman 女の子 can be affectionate or patronizing depending on context. Calling her 女 flatly is almost always too blunt.

The kanji 男おとこ is 田た (rice field) over 力ちから (power/strength). Power in the rice field. For thousands of years, this was the definition of maleness across East Asia — not warrior strength but agricultural labor, the muscle that feeds the family. The character hasn't changed, even as the job description has.

男の子 works the same way as 女の子: 男おとこ + の + 子こ = a male child, a boy. Like its counterpart, the age range is flexible. かわいい男おとこの子こ could describe a toddler or a twenty-year-old, depending on who's talking. The word is inherently warm and light — never formal, never harsh.

Register matters: 男の子 (casual, affectionate) → 男おとこの人ひと (neutral polite) → 男性だんせい (formal) → 男おとこ (blunt, rough). The formal 男性 uses the Chinese-derived reading だん; the casual words use native おとこ. In Japanese, Chinese readings almost always signal higher formality. Keep that principle and you can predict register across hundreds of words.

Japanese makes plurals by adding suffixes. 私わたし (I) + たち = we. Same たち works on anything: 子供こどもたち (children), 彼かれたち (they). The word 友達ともだち (friend) already has 達たち — the kanji form of たち — baked in.

たち isn't the only option. ら is rougher: 僕ぼくら (we — masculine casual), 俺おれら (we — masculine rough). たち is softer, polite, works everywhere.

And just like 私 alone, 私たち gets dropped when the group reference is obvious. Japanese prefers implicit subjects. 明日あした行いく over 私わたしたちは明日あした行いく when it's clear you mean the group. Using 私たち explicitly adds emphasis — "we are the ones going" — or claims collective identity: 私わたしたちの学校がっこう (our school).

見方 is literally 見み (see) + 方かた (way, method) — your way of seeing. Not what you see, but how you see it. 見方みかたを変かえる (change your perspective) is one of those phrases that shows up in every self-help book in Japan, but it's also perfectly natural in daily conversation: 見方みかたによっては正ただしい (depending on how you look at it, it's correct).

Watch the pitch: みかた with this kanji means "viewpoint." But there's a homophone — 味方みかた, also みかた, meaning "ally" or "someone on your side." Context always makes it obvious: if you're talking about opinions, it's 見方. If you're talking about people who've got your back, it's 味方みかた. In writing the kanji settles it instantly, but in speech you might get a split second of ambiguity that resolves as the sentence unfolds.

The 〜方 pattern is productive. 食たべ方かた (way of eating), 使つかい方かた (way of using), 考かんがえ方かた (way of thinking). Once you recognize it, 見方 stops being a vocabulary word and becomes a predictable compound.

The kanji 鳥 is a picture of a bird. Not metaphorically — literally. The oracle bone form was a bird in profile: head, beak, body, tail feathers, legs. Over 3,000 years of simplification turned it into the modern character, but you can still trace the silhouette if you squint. The four dots at the bottom are the tail feathers. The horizontal stroke near the top is the eye.

There's a related kanji you should know: 鳥 vs 烏からす (crow). Notice that 烏 is missing the horizontal stroke that represents the eye. The folk explanation: crows are so black you can't see their eyes. Whether or not that's the real etymology, it's impossible to forget once you've heard it.

とり in everyday Japanese does double duty. It means "bird" generically, but in the kitchen it means "chicken" — 鳥肉とりにく (chicken meat), or just 鳥 at a yakitori stand. A restaurant menu saying 鳥 isn't offering sparrow.

犬 is 大だい (big, a person with arms spread) with a dot. That dot is the dog — or more precisely, it turns the person into a four-legged animal. The oldest forms showed a complete dog in profile, but as the character simplified over millennia, it collapsed into "person-shape plus one distinguishing mark." The dot is all that separates dog from big. Miss it when writing and you've got a completely different character.

This kanji shows up as a radical in dozens of animal and beast-related characters: 狼おおかみ (wolf), 猫ねこ (cat), 猿さる (monkey), 獣けもの (beast). When you see 犭 (the squished version of 犬) on the left side of a kanji, expect an animal.

The word いぬ is emotionally neutral in a way that might surprise you. Japanese has warmer alternatives: ワンちゃん is the baby-talk/affectionate version (from the onomatopoeia ワンワン, a dog's bark in Japanese). Calling someone's beloved pet 犬 to their face isn't rude, but ワンちゃん shows you care.

返へん (return) + 事じ (thing, matter). A "returned thing" — you got a question, now you're sending something back. Simple etymology, but the word carries more social weight than English "reply" does.

In Japanese households and classrooms, 返事 specifically means answering when called. A parent calls your name — 返事へんじは？ means "where's your answer?" i.e., "say something when I call you." The expected response is はい. Not answering when spoken to — giving no 返事 — is a distinct kind of rudeness in Japan, especially from children. 返事へんじをしなさい (answer me!) is something every Japanese kid has heard.

It's a する-noun: 返事へんじする means "to reply." メールの返事へんじがまだ来こない (the reply to my email still hasn't come) uses it as a plain noun. For written or more formal contexts, 返答へんとう or 回答かいとう exist, but 返事 is the one you'll reach for every day.

また is the word Japanese uses to say goodbye without saying goodbye. またね — "again, right?" — means "see you." It promises a next time without specifying when. また明日あした (see you tomorrow). また来週らいしゅう (see you next week). The word faces forward: we'll do this again.

As an adverb, it simply means "again": また失敗しっぱいした (I failed again). また雨あめだ (rain again). But また also works as a conjunction meaning "also" or "and" in more formal writing: 彼かれは医者いしゃであり、また作家さっかでもある (he is a doctor and also a writer). This usage is stiffer than the conversational "again" sense.

No kanji in common use. The kanji 又また exists but you'll almost never see また written that way in modern text. It stays in hiragana, which gives it a soft, casual feel — fitting for a word that's fundamentally friendly.

meaning: period of 1 year, per year, annual
lesson:
年ねん (year) + 間かん (interval, span). Where 年 alone is just "year," 年間 emphasizes the duration — the full stretch of time from start to finish. 三年間さんねんかん (a period of three years) vs 三年さんねん (three years). The difference is subtle: 三年 can mean "year three" or just "three years," while 三年間 unambiguously means "for the span of three years."

You'll see this 〜間 suffix on other time words: 時間じかん (time/hours), 週間しゅうかん (weeks), 期間きかん (period). It always adds that sense of measured duration — you're counting how long something stretches, not just labeling a point in time.

年間売上ねんかんうりあげ (annual sales). 年間ねんかんパスポート (annual pass). When 年間 modifies a noun directly, it means "yearly" or "annual" — the entire year treated as a single unit.

青 is the color that refuses to pick a lane. It covers what English calls blue and what English calls green, depending on context. The sky is 青い. The sea is 青い. But so is the "go" light at an intersection — 青信号あおしんごう (green light) — even though it's objectively green. Unripe fruit? 青い. A pale, sickly face? 顔かおが青あおい. Young and inexperienced? 青い.

This isn't a quirk of Japanese. Ancient languages worldwide tended to group blue and green under one term. Japanese eventually added 緑みどり for green, but 青 never gave up its territory. Traffic lights, vegetables, lush mountains — anything that was 青 before 緑 existed stayed 青. The word carries its history in its usage.

The kanji 青 originally depicted a plant (生せい, life/growth) emerging with a vivid color — the intense blue-green of new growth. That root meaning of "vivid, fresh" is why 青 extends to youth (青春せいしゅん, youth — literally "blue spring") and inexperience (青二才あおにさい, greenhorn).

The kanji 赤 is 大だい (big, a person) over 火ひ (fire). A person over flames — and what color do you see? The character is its own mnemonic. Some scholars trace it to the reddened skin of someone standing near fire, others to the color of the flames themselves. Either way: person + fire = red.

赤 does more than name a color. 赤あかちゃん means "baby" — not because babies are red (though newborns are), but because 赤 carries a connotation of "bare, raw, undisguised." A 赤あかちゃん is a naked little being with nothing to hide. 赤あかの他人たにん (a complete stranger) uses the same logic — someone with absolutely no connection to you. 赤 intensifies: 赤字あかじ (red ink = financial deficit), just like English "in the red."

As an adjective, you'll use 赤あかい. As a noun — the color itself — it's just 赤. The two coexist: 赤あかい花はな (a red flower) vs 赤あかが好すき (I like red).

If you ask someone for directions in Japan, the answer will almost certainly involve this word. 次つぎの信号しんごうを右みぎに曲まがって (turn right at the next traffic light). 信号 is the landmark that organizes Japanese navigation — intersections with traffic lights are the waypoints everything else is measured from.

The kanji: 信しん (trust) + 号ごう (sign, call). A trusted sign. The word technically covers any signal — radio signals, smoke signals — but in daily life it means traffic light, full stop. 信号しんごうが赤あかだ (the light is red). 信号しんごうを無視むしする (run a red light).

The green light is 青信号あおしんごう — a "blue signal," not 緑信号. This is 青's territory. Yellow is 黄信号きしんごう. Red is 赤信号あかしんごう. And there's a proverb: 赤信号あかしんごう、みんなで渡わたれば怖こわくない (a red light isn't scary if everyone crosses together) — a darkly funny commentary on Japanese group mentality.

This is the second entry for 円 in the list (it appeared earlier as a basic vocabulary item), and both meanings — "yen" and "circle" — trace back to the same root. The Japanese currency was named 円 because the first modern coins were round, and 円 means "round" or "circle." The character originally depicted something enclosed and complete — a perfect shape with no edges.

As currency: 百円ひゃくえん (100 yen), 千円せんえん (1,000 yen). No plural, no counter word needed — just number + 円. It's one of the simplest counters in the language. いくら？三百円さんびゃくえん。 (How much? 300 yen.)

The "circle" meaning lives mostly in compounds: 円形えんけい (circular shape), 円満えんまん (harmonious — a well-rounded relationship). By itself, まる or 丸まる is more common for "circle" in everyday speech. But knowing that 円 means round gives 円満 and 円滑えんかつ (smooth) an intuitive feel: things that are round roll smoothly.

非ひ (non-, un-) + 常じょう (ordinary, normal) + に. "In a not-ordinary way." That etymology tells you everything about when to use it: 非常に is formal, slightly stiff, and carries more weight than とても. A news anchor says 非常に. A professor says 非常に. Your friend at a bar says とても or めっちゃ.

非常ひじょうに難むずかしい問題もんだい (an extremely difficult problem). 非常ひじょうに重要じゅうようです (it is extremely important). The word pairs naturally with weighty adjectives and formal contexts. Using it to describe your lunch would sound comically stiff — like saying "exceedingly" in English to describe a sandwich.

非常 without the に works as a noun meaning "emergency": 非常口ひじょうぐち (emergency exit), 非常事態ひじょうじたい (state of emergency). Same kanji, same root idea — something beyond the ordinary — but one is an adverb of degree and the other is a noun of crisis.

複ふく (duplicate, multiple) + 雑ざつ (miscellaneous, mixed). Multiple things mixed together — complicated. The kanji practically define the word: when many different elements get tangled up, you've got 複雑.

This word describes situations, not puzzles. A math problem is 難むずかしい (difficult). A bureaucratic process with seventeen forms, three offices, and contradictory rules is 複雑. Human relationships are 複雑. 複雑ふくざつな気持きもち (complicated feelings) is something Japanese people say constantly — when you're happy and sad at the same time, when something good happened but it cost something, when you can't name a single clean emotion. It's one of the most emotionally honest phrases in the language.

As a な-adjective: 複雑ふくざつな問題もんだい (a complex problem). As a noun with する: 事態じたいが複雑化ふくざつかする (the situation becomes complicated) — the 化か suffix turns it into a verb of transformation.

Dictionaryふくざつ（な）
Negativeふくざつじゃない
Pastふくざつだった
Past negふくざつじゃなかった
Adverbふくざつに
て-formふくざつで

平 (flat, level, calm) + 和 (harmony, Japan). A flat, harmonious state — peace. Both kanji carry enormous cultural weight in Japan. 平 is in 平成へいせい (the Heisei era, 1989-2019). 和 is in 令和れいわ (the current era) and is one of the kanji used to refer to Japan itself (和食わしょく = Japanese food, 和室わしつ = Japanese-style room).

平和 carries particular weight in postwar Japan. Hiroshima's Peace Memorial Park is 平和記念公園へいわきねんこうえん. Article 9 of the Japanese constitution — the clause renouncing war — is called the 平和条項へいわじょうこう. The word isn't just a dictionary entry. It's tied to a specific national wound and the resolve that followed.

In lighter usage: 平和へいわな一日いちにち (a peaceful day). 今日きょうは平和へいわだった (today was uneventful/peaceful). Young people sometimes use it sarcastically — 平和へいわだな〜 when nothing's happening, when life is boringly calm. The な-adjective usage is straightforward.

Dictionaryへいわ（な）
Negativeへいわじゃない
Pastへいわだった
Past negへいわじゃなかった
Adverbへいわに
て-formへいわで

meaning: to turn around, go around, rotate, revolve
lesson:
The kanji 回 is a visual loop — a square spiral, something turning in on itself. The oldest forms showed a whirlpool. Rotation is baked into the character's shape.

回る covers an enormous range of circular motion. A top spins: コマが回る. The earth revolves: 地球が回る. You tour a city: 街を回る. Gossip gets around: 噂が回る. Your head spins when you're dizzy: 頭が回る. The connecting thread is always circular movement — something going around, making a circuit, completing a loop.

Pair: 回る (intransitive, things spin on their own) vs 回す (まわす, transitive, you spin something). ハンドルを回す (turn the steering wheel). 順番を回す (pass the turn along). The す ending is your signal that someone is doing the spinning.

Particles: As an intransitive motion verb, 回る takes を for the path or place you go around — 公園を回る (go around the park). This を marks the route, not a direct object. For "spin" uses, the spinning thing is the subject with が.

Passive に回られる: someone going around you, outflanking you — 敵に回られた (the enemy got around me). に marks who did the circling.

Causative を／に回らせる: making someone go around. を for coercive — 新人を回らせる (make the new hire do the rounds). に for permissive.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 回す (まわす)

若 (young) + 者 (person). Transparent compound, but the word has a specific register: it's how older people and the media refer to young people as a group. News headlines love 若者: 若者の投票率 (youth voter turnout), 若者離れ (young people drifting away from something). That last pattern — 若者の〇〇離れ — is a perennial Japanese media cliche. Young people leaving behind beer, cars, TV, marriage. Every generation gets this headline.

A young person wouldn't normally call themselves 若者. It's a third-person label, slightly clinical, the way "youth" sounds in English. You'd say 若い人 in casual conversation, or just refer to specific age groups. 若者 is the word you hear in discussions about young people, not from them.

The 者 suffix is productive: 医者 (doctor), 学者 (scholar), 記者 (reporter). It always marks "a person who is or does [X]." Here, a person who is young.

The kanji 雪 has 雨 (rain) on top — the radical for all weather that falls from the sky (雲 cloud, 雷 thunder, 霧 fog). Below it, ヨ, which in this character represented a hand sweeping — the idea of wiping clean. Snow covers and blankets everything, erasing the landscape. Rain that cleans the world white.

Japan has a deep relationship with snow. The country's western coast — the 日本海 side — gets buried under some of the heaviest snowfall on Earth. 雪国 (ゆきぐに, snow country) is both a geographic term for these regions and the title of Kawabata's famous novel. 雪祭り (ゆきまつり, snow festival) — Sapporo's annual event — draws millions.

The sound ゆき is soft, almost whispered — yu-ki, two gentle syllables. It happens to sound nothing like "snow" in English, but if you try to say it quietly, it has the hushed quality of watching snowfall. A useful coincidence for anchoring the sound to the meaning.

This is the second うまい in the list — the first (word 167) meant "good at, skilled." Same word, different face. うまい means both "skillful" and "tasty," and the connection isn't accidental. The original meaning was something like "satisfying" or "well-done" — food that's well-made tastes good, and a person who does things well is skilled. The word branched but never fully split.

In the "delicious" sense, うまい is casual and slightly masculine. このラーメン、うまい！ is something you'd hear at a ramen shop from a guy slurping noodles. It's earthier than おいしい, which is gender-neutral and appropriate everywhere. Women certainly use うまい too — the gender skew is real but not a rule.

The kanji 旨い exists but is rarely used. うまい almost always appears in hiragana, which keeps it looking informal — matching its casual register.

Dictionaryうまい
Negativeうまくない
Pastうまかった
Past negうまくなかった
Adverbうまく
て-formうまくて

思い (thought, feeling) + 出す (to take out, put out). You reach into your memory and pull a thought out. That's the physical metaphor: memories are stored inside you, and 思い出す is the act of extracting one. English says "recall" — to call back. Japanese says "think-out" — to push the thought outward into consciousness.

Don't confuse this with 覚える (おぼえる, to memorize). 覚える is putting information in. 思い出す is getting it back out. 単語を覚える (memorize a word). 昔のことを思い出す (remember something from long ago). One is storage, the other is retrieval.

The noun form 思い出 (おもいで) — without the す — means "memory" or "recollection," always with a warm, nostalgic tinge. いい思い出 (a good memory). It's the word you'd use in a graduation speech or looking at old photos. The verb pulls the memory out; the noun is what you're left holding.

Particles: 思い出す takes を — the memory is the direct object you're extracting. 名前を思い出す (remember a name). 子供の頃を思い出す (remember childhood).

Potential が思い出せる: が marks what you can recall — 名前が思い出せない (I can't remember the name). This negative potential form is extremely common — you use it when a memory is stuck on the tip of your tongue.

Passive に思い出される: rare. Memories being recalled is not typically framed as something done to you.

Causative に思い出させる: に marks who you remind. 彼女に昔のことを思い出させた (I reminded her of the old days).

聞く is what you do on purpose — listen, ask. 聞こえる is what happens to you — sound reaches your ears whether you want it to or not. The difference is agency. 音楽を聞く (listen to music — you chose to) vs 音楽が聞こえる (music can be heard — it's just there). The が is your clue: the sound is the subject, acting on you.

This pairs with 見える (to be visible) — another involuntary perception verb. 見る/見える for sight, 聞く/聞こえる for sound. Active/passive perception. Japanese draws this line consistently where English often doesn't.

声が聞こえる？ (can you hear my voice?) — the most common usage. You're asking if sound is reaching someone, not if they're trying to listen. 何も聞こえない (I can't hear anything). 隣の部屋から音が聞こえる (I can hear sounds from the next room).

Particles: 聞こえる takes が — the sound is the subject, not something you act on. 声が聞こえる (a voice can be heard). Direction can be marked with から — 外から音が聞こえる.

Passive: 聞こえる is already passive-like in meaning, so a separate passive form is essentially never used.

Causative に聞こえさせる: extremely rare. If you want to "make something heard," you'd use 聞かせる (the causative of 聞く) instead.

Cond ～たらきこえたらが

English has "borrow" and "rent" as completely different concepts. Japanese doesn't care about the distinction. 借りる covers both — you're temporarily using something that belongs to someone else. 本を借りる (borrow a book). アパートを借りる (rent an apartment). お金を借りる (borrow money). Whether money changes hands is irrelevant to the Japanese word. What matters is that the thing goes back eventually.

Its partner is 貸す (かす, to lend). 借りる/貸す always describe the same transaction from opposite sides: I 借りる from you, you 貸す to me. Mixing them up is a classic mistake — just remember that 借りる is the receiver's verb.

The kanji 借 has the person radical (亻) on the left — a person doing the borrowing — and 昔 (the past, long ago) on the right, used here for its phonetic value. But there's an accidental poetry: borrowing links you to the past, because you owe something that has to come back.

Particles: 借りる takes を for the thing borrowed — 本を借りる. The person you borrow from takes に or から — 友達に借りる or 友達から借りる.

Potential が借りられる: が — what you can borrow becomes the subject. ここでDVDが借りられる (you can rent DVDs here).

Passive に借りられる: に marks the borrower. 弟に車を借りられた (my brother borrowed my car — and I'm not happy about it).

Causative に…を借りさせる: に marks who you make borrow. 子供に図書館で本を借りさせる (have the kid borrow a book from the library).

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 貸す (かす)

Politeかりますを
Polite negかりませんを
Pastかりたを
Polite pastかりましたを
て-formかりてを
Volitionalかりようを

Passiveかりられるに
Causativeかりさせるに／を

Potentialかりられるが
Imperativeかりろを
Imp なさいかりなさいを
Cond ～ばかりればを
Cond ～たらかりたらを

返す completes the circuit that 借りる starts. You borrowed it, now you give it back. 本を返す (return a book). お金を返す (pay back money). The kanji 返 has the movement radical 辶 (road, movement) with 反 (turn, reverse) — something going back the way it came.

Don't confuse 返す (かえす) with 帰る (かえる, to go home). Same sound family, different words. 返す is about returning things. 帰る is about returning yourself — going home. The す ending on 返す is your clue that it's transitive: you're acting on an object.

返す also shows up in compound verbs where it means "do back" or "reverse": 裏返す (うらがえす, turn inside out), 言い返す (いいかえす, talk back, retort), 繰り返す (くりかえす, repeat — turn over again). The 〜返す pattern always carries a sense of reversal or repetition.

Particles: 返す takes を for the thing returned — 本を返す. The person you return it to takes に — 図書館に返す (return to the library).

Potential が返せる: が — what you can return becomes the subject. お金が返せない (I can't pay it back).

Passive に返される: に marks who returned it. プレゼントを返された (my present was returned to me — rejection).

Causative に…を返させる: に marks who you make return something. 弟に本を返させる (make my brother return the book).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 返る (かえる)

受け (receive, catch) + 取る (take). You receive and take — a double action that emphasizes physically getting your hands on something. This isn't abstract receiving like getting praise or an impression. 受け取る is concrete: 荷物を受け取る (pick up a package), 給料を受け取る (receive your salary), 手紙を受け取る (receive a letter).

There's also a figurative extension: to take something a certain way, to interpret. 冗談として受け取る (take it as a joke). 悪く受け取らないで (don't take it the wrong way). The physical metaphor still applies — you're "catching" the meaning in a particular way.

Neighborhood: もらう (receive from someone, with gratitude implied), 受ける (receive in a broader/more abstract sense — exams, impressions, surgery), 受け取る (physically receive and take possession). When a delivery person hands you a box, that's 受け取る.

Particles: 受け取る takes を for the thing received — 荷物を受け取る. The source takes から — 配達員から荷物を受け取る (receive a package from the delivery person).

Potential が受け取れる: が marks what you can receive. 今日中に荷物が受け取れる (I can pick up the package today).

Passive に受け取られる: に marks the receiver. 皮肉として受け取られた (it was taken as sarcasm).

Causative に受け取らせる: に marks who you make receive. 部下に書類を受け取らせる (have a subordinate pick up the documents).

The kanji 捨 is the hand radical (扌) plus 舎 — a building, a shelter. A hand pushing something out of the house. You're throwing it away, letting it go, done with it.

捨てる is blunt. It means to throw away, discard, dump — physically or metaphorically. ゴミを捨てる (throw away trash). 古い服を捨てる (toss old clothes). But it scales up: 夢を捨てる (abandon a dream). 子供を捨てる (abandon a child). The harshness doesn't soften with abstract usage. If someone says 捨てられた about a relationship, it means they were discarded — tossed aside like garbage. It stings.

A useful set phrase: 捨てたもんじゃない — literally "it's not something to throw away," meaning "it's not bad, it has value, don't dismiss it." A backhanded compliment structure. 日本の冬も捨てたもんじゃない (Japanese winter isn't so bad, actually).

Particles: 捨てる takes を — the thing you throw away is your direct object. ゴミを捨てる. Where you throw it takes に — ゴミ箱に捨てる (throw it in the trash can).

Potential が捨てられる: が — what you can throw away. 思い出は捨てられない (I can't throw away the memories).

Passive に捨てられる: に marks who did the discarding. 彼に捨てられた (he dumped me). One of the more emotionally loaded passives in the language.

Causative に…を捨てさせる: に marks who you make discard. 子供にいらないおもちゃを捨てさせる (make the kid throw away toys they don't need).

一 (one) + 緒 (thread, beginning). One thread — things bound together on a single line. The kanji 緒 has the thread radical (糸), reinforcing the image of connection.

The most common pattern is 一緒に — together, as in doing something alongside someone. 一緒に行こう (let's go together). 一緒に食べない？ (want to eat together?). This is the word you reach for when inviting someone to join you. It's warm and inclusive — the verbal equivalent of scooting over to make room.

But 一緒 also means "same": 私と一緒だ (it's the same as me / it's the same as mine). 味は一緒 (the taste is the same). The two meanings — "together" and "same" — collapse into one idea: things that are together become the same, and things that are the same belong together.

The noun form of 遊ぶ (あそぶ, to play). Strip off the ぶ, add nothing — 遊び is what you get when you nominalize the verb. Play itself. Fun as a thing.

Japanese 遊び has a wider range than English "play." Children play, yes — 子供の遊び. But adults have 遊び too: going out, entertainment, leisure activities. 遊びに行く (go out to have fun / go hang out) is how you describe meeting friends for no particular productive purpose. There's no childish connotation. It's the opposite of work.

遊び also has a mechanical meaning: slack, play, looseness in a machine or mechanism. ハンドルに遊びがある (there's play in the steering wheel). The conceptual bridge: play is the space where things aren't locked down tight, where there's room to move freely. Freedom of movement — whether it's kids in a park or a loose bolt — is 遊び.

移す means to move something from one place to another — a deliberate transfer. 荷物を別の部屋に移す (move the luggage to another room). データを新しいパソコンに移す (transfer data to a new computer). It implies purposeful relocation, not just general movement.

The kanji 移 has the grain radical (禾) on the left — think rice plants — and 多 (many) on the right. Transplanting seedlings, moving many plants from one paddy to another. Agriculture is the origin of this "transfer" meaning.

Pair: 移す (transitive — you move something) vs 移る (うつる, intransitive — something moves on its own). 会社を東京に移す (move the company to Tokyo — you're doing it) vs 会社が東京に移る (the company moves to Tokyo — it just happens). There's also a secondary meaning for both: spreading. 風邪を移す (spread a cold to someone). 風邪が移る (catch a cold from someone).

Particles: 移す takes を for the thing moved — 視線を移す (shift one's gaze). Destination takes に.

Potential が移せる: が — what you can move. データが移せない (I can't transfer the data).

Passive に移される: に marks who did the moving. 別の部署に移された (I was transferred to a different department).

Causative に移させる: making someone transfer something. 部下にファイルを移させる (have the subordinate move the files).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 移る (うつる)

Take 大きい (big), drop the い, add さ — you get the abstract noun: bigness, size. This 〜さ suffix is one of the most useful tools in Japanese word-building. It turns any い-adjective into a measurable noun: 高い → 高さ (height), 長い → 長さ (length), 重い → 重さ (weight). Every time you see 〜さ on the end of a word, you're looking at a quality turned into a quantity.

大きさ is neutral — it doesn't mean "largeness." It means "size" in general. 大きさはどのくらい？ (how big is it? / what's the size?) can apply to anything from an ant to a building. 同じ大きさ (the same size). 手のひらの大きさ (the size of a palm).

meaning: thought, idea, plan, way of thinking
lesson:
The noun form of 考える (かんがえる, to think deliberately). Where 考える is the verb — the process of working something out in your head — 考え is the product: the thought itself, the idea you arrived at, the plan you formed.

いい考えだ (that's a good idea). 考えが変わった (my thinking changed / I changed my mind). あなたの考えは？ (what do you think? — literally, what's your thought?). It can mean a deliberate plan: 何か考えがあるの？ (do you have something in mind?).

Notice the difference from 思い (おもい), which is a feeling-thought, emotional and instinctive. 考え is a reasoning-thought, deliberate and analytical. The same split that separates the parent verbs 思う and 考える carries over into their noun forms. Your 考え is something you can explain and defend. Your 思い is something you feel and can't always articulate.

空 (sky, empty) + 港 (port, harbor). A port in the sky. Before airports existed, 港 meant a seaport — boats coming and going. When air travel arrived, Japanese simply swapped the element: sea-port became sky-port. The logic is clean and the word is instantly memorable once you see the pieces.

空港に着く (arrive at the airport). 空港まで送る (take someone to the airport). 成田空港 (Narita Airport). In Japan, the airport name always comes first, then 空港: 羽田空港, 関西空港.

The kanji 港 has the water radical (氵) because it originally referred exclusively to water harbors. Combining it with 空 repurposes it for air travel — a nice example of how Japanese builds new vocabulary by recombining existing kanji rather than borrowing from English. (Though エアポート also exists as a loanword, 空港 is far more common.)

Every journey in Japanese starts with 出発 and ends with 到着 (とうちゃく, arrival). These are the two words on every airport display board, every train schedule, every bus timetable. 出発の時間だ (it's time to leave). 出発が遅れた (the departure was delayed).

Both kanji push outward: 出 (go out) + 発 (emit, launch). The word has a kinetic quality that 出る alone doesn't — 出発 implies momentum, a deliberate setting-off. 九時に出発する (depart at nine).

出発点 (しゅっぱつてん, starting point) extends the word into metaphor. ここが出発点だ (this is where it all starts). A career, a project, a relationship — any beginning can be a 出発点. The word doesn't just mean leaving. It means launching toward something.
